narrative_summary:
The RAD51C c.577C>T (p.Arg193Ter) variant is a nonsense substitution that creates a premature stop codon in the RAD51C DNA‑repair protein and has been reported in individuals with hereditary breast‑ovarian cancer syndromes and other malignancies such as pancreatic and gynecologic cancers [PMC6389551][PMC11592649].
Clinical observations across multiple cohorts consistently classify this allele as pathogenic. It was identified in a patient with multifocal paraganglioma and monoclonal B‑cell lymphocytosis [PMC6389551], in Polish ovarian‑cancer cases at a frequency of 0.48% with no carriers among controls (OR≈6.3) [PMC7921976], in Southern Italian high‑risk breast‑cancer patients as a pathogenic variant [PMC11592649], and in extended cancer‑predisposition panels where it was labeled class 5 (pathogenic) in hereditary breast‑ovarian cancer cohorts [PMC7821123]. Additional reports describe the same variant in pancreatic cancer [PMC6020441], Chinese gynecologic cancer patients [PMC10391307], and Swedish hereditary cancer screening where it contributed to diagnostic yield [PMC10413543].
Functional data demonstrate that the germline truncating c.577C>T allele abolishes RAD51C function, conferring initial sensitivity to the PARP inhibitor rucaparib; secondary somatic mutations that restore the open reading frame were observed in progressing disease, indicating that loss of RAD51C activity drives therapeutic response and resistance [PMC5612362].
Population databases show the variant is extremely rare, with a non‑Finnish European allele frequency of ~1.2 × 10⁻⁵ and no detectable frequency in East Asian cohorts, supporting its rarity in the general population [PMC11211533]. Computational annotation uniformly predicts a stop‑gain (nonsense) consequence, consistent with a loss‑of‑function effect [PMC5289469].
Taken together, the convergent clinical classifications, functional loss‑of‑function evidence, very low population frequency, and consistent in‑silico predictions support the interpretation of RAD51C c.577C>T (p.Arg193Ter) as a pathogenic variant that increases risk for breast, ovarian, and related cancers.

article_mentions.overall_article_summary: The study shows that a germline truncating RAD51C c.577C>T mutation initially confers sensitivity to the PARP inhibitor rucaparib, while secondary mutations that restore the open reading frame arise in progressing disease and mediate acquired resistance.
article_mentions.title: Secondary Somatic Mutations Restoring RAD51C and RAD51D Associated with Acquired Resistance to the PARP Inhibitor Rucaparib in High-Grade Ovarian Carcinoma

mentions.quoted_text: Peripheral blood flow cytometry found a population of CD5+ lambda-monotypic B cells (6%), consistent with the immunophenotype of chronic lymphocytic leukemia/small lymphocytic lymphoma. Based on a white blood cell count of 7.5 x 109 cells/per milliliter, low-count monoclonal B-cell lymphocytosis was favored. Computed tomography of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis found right-sided abdominal/retroperitoneal enhancing masses: one 5.5- x 4.9-cm invading the caudate lobe of the liver and effacing the portal vein, and one 5.2- x 4.2-cm mass anterior to the inferior vena cava, consistent with enlargement of her known malignant multifocal paragangliomas. The patient opted to treat her intraabdominal paragangliomas and her monoclonal B-cell lymphocytosis with observation. Given the diagnosis with 2 malignancies and a monoclonal B-cell lymphocytosis, as well as a family history of ovarian and colon cancer, genetic testing was performed via the CustomNext (PGLNext and OvaNext) panel (Ambry Genetics). She was found to be positive for the RAD51C **c.577C>T** (p.R193*) mutation.

article_mentions.overall_article_summary: The article describes a patient with multifocal paraganglioma, mycosis fungoides, and monoclonal B‑cell lymphocytosis who was found to carry the RAD51C c.577C>T (p.R193*) truncating variant.
article_mentions.title: Multifocal paraganglioma, mycosis fungoides, and monoclonal B-cell lymphocytosis in association with RAD51C mutation

article_mentions.overall_article_summary: The study finds that most pathogenic variants in DNA‑damage‑repair genes, including the RAD51C c.577C>T stop‑gain, are low‑frequency and likely arose after the major out‑of‑Africa migration, highlighting recent evolutionary origins of DDR‑related disease risk.
article_mentions.title: Pathogenic variants in human DNA damage repair genes mostly arose after the latest human out-of-Africa migration

article_mentions.overall_article_summary: The study reports that the RAD51C c.577C>T (p.Arg193Ter) variant was observed in the cohort and classified as pathogenic in their multigene panel testing results.
article_mentions.title: Pathogenic and likely pathogenic variant prevalence among the first 10,000 patients referred for next-generation cancer panel testing

article_mentions.overall_article_summary: The study shows that whole‑exome sequencing provides adequate coverage for >98% of pathogenic variants, including RAD51C c.577C>T, suggesting WES can achieve diagnostic yields comparable to targeted panels.
article_mentions.title: Exome sequencing covers >98% of mutations identified on targeted next generation sequencing panels
